paragraphs.text:
## SRD-46 survey: Fe(II) vs Fe(III) ligand systems

SRD-46 coverage is strongly skewed toward **Fe(III)**. In discovery, the database resolved **Fe(III) = `metal_61`** and **Fe(II) = `metal_62`**; the Fe(III) catalog showed many representative systems, while Fe(II) was much sparser in the top returned systems. The Fe(III) catalog includes hydroxide, acetate, oxalate, EDTA/NTA/CDTA-type aminopolycarboxylates, salicylates, Tiron/catecholate-type ligands, 8-hydroxyquinoline, hydroxamates, fluoride, chloride, and thiocyanate.  
Source: **NIST SRD-46** (`build_system_catalog`, `search_networks`, `search_pka_ligands`).

SRD-46 coverage is strongly skewed toward **Fe(III)**.
In discovery, the database resolved
Fe(III) = `metal_61`
Fe(II) = `metal_62`
the Fe(III) catalog showed many representative systems
Fe(II) was much sparser in the top returned systems.

The Fe(III) catalog includes hydroxide, acetate, oxalate, EDTA/NTA/CDTA-type aminopolycarboxylates, salicylates, Tiron/catecholate-type ligands, 8-hydroxyquinoline, hydroxamates, fluoride, chloride, and thiocyanate.
Source: **NIST SRD-46** (`build_system_catalog`, `search_networks`, `search_pka_ligands`).

paragraphs.text:
### What the database directly supports

The strongest *numerical* stability data retrieved in this pass came from the **Fe(III) hydroxide network**, which is useful as an anchor because it shows how strongly Fe(III) favors hard oxygen donors and how quickly that preference turns into hydrolysis/polymerization rather than simple soluble ML species.

| Fe(III) + hydroxide species | Equation form in SRD-46 | logK / logβ | T range (°C) | I range (M) |
|---|---|---:|---:|---:|
| ML | `[M] + [L] <=> [ML]` | 11.27 | 20–30 | ~0–0.25 |
| ML2 | `[M] + [L]^2 <=> [ML2]` | 23.4 | 20–30 | ~0–0.25 |
| M2L2 | `[M]^2 + [L]^2 <=> [M2L2]` | 25.14 | 20–30 | ~0–0.25 |
| ML4 | `[M] + [L]^4 <=> [ML4]` | 34.4 | 20–30 | ~0–0.25 |
| M3L4 | `[M]^3 + [L]^4 <=> [M3L4]` | 49.7 | 20–30 | ~0–0.25 |

These same hydroxide networks also include solid/hydrolyzed forms:

| Fe(III) hydrolysis / precipitation entry | Equation form in SRD-46 | logK | T range (°C) |
|---|---|---:|---:|
| FeOOH(s)-type | `[MOOH(s,alpha)] + [H2O] <=> [M] + [OH]^3` | -41.5 | 20–30 |
| Fe2O3(s)-type | `[(M2O3)0.5(s,alpha)] + [H2O]^1.5 <=> [M] + [OH]^3` | -42.7 | 20–30 |
| ML3(s)-type hydroxide solid entry | `[ML3(s)] <=> [M] + [L]^3` | -38.8 | 20–30 |

This is the clearest database-backed edge case: **Fe(III) binds O donors so strongly that aqueous coordination chemistry quickly merges into hydrolysis, condensation, and precipitation**.

paragraphs.text:
## Ligand classes present in SRD-46 for Fe(III)

From the Fe(III) system catalog:

| Ligand class | Representative Fe(III) systems seen in SRD-46 |
|---|---|
| Hard O donors / hydrolysis | hydroxide |
| Simple carboxylates | acetate, malonate, oxalate |
| Aminopolycarboxylates | EDTA, NTA, CDTA |
| Salicylate / phenolate-type O,O ligands | salicylate, p-aminosalicylate, Tiron |
| Mixed N,O chelates | 8-hydroxyquinoline |
| Hydroxamates | acetohydroxamate and related hydroxamate ligands |
| Halides / pseudohalides | fluoride, chloride, thiocyanate, azide |

paragraphs.text:
## Protonation data that helps interpret the ordering

The pKa search confirms that many of the strongest Fe(III)-binding ligands are only powerful after partial or full **deprotonation**, i.e. once they become hard, anionic donors.

| Ligand | SRD-46 ligand ID | Acid-base form shown | Representative pKa values retrieved |
|---|---|---|---|
| Salicylic acid | ligand_9257 | H2L | 2.8; phenolic deprotonation reported at very high basicity in SRD-46 ladder |
| 8-Hydroxyquinoline | ligand_8126 | HL | 4.97, 9.65 |
| Acetohydroxamic acid | ligand_9873 | HL | 9.29 |
| Tiron | ligand_9358 | H4L | 7.62 plus very strong acidic sulfonate-related ladder entries |
| Acetohydroxamate derivative | ligand_5938 | H2L | 6.99, 9.09 |
| NTA-family examples | various | H3L/H4L | many values in the ~2–12 range depending on substitution |

paragraphs.text:
## Trend across ligand classes

A database-supported qualitative ordering is:

| Approximate Fe(III) stability tendency | Ligand classes | Main reason |
|---|---|---|
| Very high | hydroxamates, catecholate/Tiron-type ligands, EDTA/NTA/CDTA-type aminopolycarboxylates | hard O donors, multiple negative charges, strong chelation |
| High | oxalate, salicylate, 8-hydroxyquinoline | chelation + deprotonated donor atoms |
| Moderate | acetate and related simple carboxylates | hard O donor, but lower denticity and less preorganization |
| Lower to moderate | chloride, thiocyanate, azide | poorer HSAB match and/or weaker monodentate binding |
| Special case | hydroxide | extremely favorable O binding, but hydrolysis/polymerization dominates |

For **Fe(II)**, this pass retrieved much less usable pairwise stability data than for Fe(III), but the database coverage itself indicates Fe(II) is less richly represented for these hard-anionic systems. The chemically consistent interpretation is:

| Class | Fe(III) vs Fe(II) trend |
|---|---|
| Hard O-donor anions and polycarboxylates | Fe(III) much more stable |
| Chelating O,O / O,N ligands | Fe(III) usually stronger |
| Neutral N donors / stronger-field ligands | Fe(II) becomes relatively more competitive |
| Soft/ambidentate ligands like SCN− | more complicated; donor mode matters |

claims.text: ## Trend across ligand classes
A database-supported qualitative ordering is:
| Very high | hydroxamates, catecholate/Tiron-type ligands, EDTA/NTA/CDTA-type aminopolycarboxylates | hard O donors, multiple negative charges, strong chelation |
| High | oxalate, salicylate, 8-hydroxyquinoline | chelation + deprotonated donor atoms |
| Moderate | acetate and related simple carboxylates | hard O donor, but lower denticity and less preorganization |
| Lower to moderate | chloride, thiocyanate, azide | poorer HSAB match and/or weaker monodentate binding |
| Special case | hydroxide | extremely favorable O binding, but hydrolysis/polymerization dominates |
For **Fe(II)**, this pass retrieved much less usable pairwise stability data than for Fe(III)
the database coverage itself indicates Fe(II) is less richly represented for these hard-anionic systems.
The chemically consistent interpretation is:
| Hard O-donor anions and polycarboxylates | Fe(III) much more stable |
| Chelating O,O / O,N ligands | Fe(III) usually stronger |
| Neutral N donors / stronger-field ligands | Fe(II) becomes relatively more competitive |
| Soft/ambidentate ligands like SCN− | more complicated; donor mode matters |

paragraphs.text:
## Thermodynamic explanation

### 1. HSAB dominates the Fe(III) ordering
**Fe(III)** is a small, highly charged, hard Lewis acid. So it strongly prefers:

| Donor type | Consequence |
|---|---|
| hard O donors | strongest stabilization |
| anionic donors | extra Coulombic stabilization |
| multidentate O-rich ligands | very large overall β values |

That explains why the Fe(III) catalog is populated by **hydroxide, oxalate, salicylates, Tiron, hydroxamates, EDTA/NTA/CDTA**.

**Fe(III)** is a small, highly charged, hard Lewis acid.
So it strongly prefers:
| hard O donors | strongest stabilization |
| anionic donors | extra Coulombic stabilization |
| multidentate O-rich ligands | very large overall β values |
That explains why
the Fe(III) catalog is populated by **hydroxide, oxalate, salicylates, Tiron, hydroxamates, EDTA/NTA/CDTA**.

paragraphs.text:
### 2. Chelate effect raises multidentate ligands above simple anions
Even if two ligands use similar donor atoms, one multidentate ligand generally outcompetes several monodentates because coordination releases more solvent molecules and reduces dissociation probability.

| Comparison | Expected result |
|---|---|
| chloride vs EDTA | EDTA far stronger |
| acetate vs oxalate | oxalate stronger |
| salicylate vs catecholate/hydroxamate-type chelators | chelating/deprotonated O-rich ligands stronger |

paragraphs.text:
### 3. Ligand basicity and deprotonation matter
The pKa data show that ligands such as **8-hydroxyquinoline, salicylate, hydroxamates, Tiron**, and aminopolycarboxylates must be deprotonated to present the strongest donor set. Once deprotonated, they become much better matched to Fe(III).

paragraphs.text:
### 4. Crystal-field effects matter more for Fe(II) edge cases
For **Fe(II)**, ligand-field stabilization can narrow the gap with Fe(III) for strong-field ligands, especially neutral N donors or π-acceptor ligands.

| Metal ion | Ligand-field role |
|---|---|
| Fe(III), often high-spin d5 | CFSE is usually a smaller driver than charge density + HSAB |
| Fe(II), d6 | stronger-field ligands can give extra stabilization and sometimes spin-state effects |

So for ligands like **bipyridine, phenanthroline, cyanide**, Fe(II) can be comparatively more competitive than it is with oxalate, EDTA, or hydroxide.

| Fe(III), often high-spin d5 | CFSE is usually a smaller driver than charge density + HSAB |
| Fe(II), d6 | stronger-field ligands can give extra stabilization and sometimes spin-state effects |
**bipyridine, phenanthroline, cyanide**
Fe(II) can be comparatively more competitive than it is with oxalate, EDTA, or hydroxide.

paragraphs.text:
## Edge cases

| Edge case | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| Hydroxide | strongest evidence in the retrieved data; Fe(III)-OH chemistry quickly becomes hydrolysis, olation/oxolation, and solids rather than simple soluble ML only |
| Thiocyanate | abundant in Fe(III) catalog despite not being a classic hard donor; ambidentate coordination and charge-transfer chemistry make it chemically prominent |
| 8-Hydroxyquinoline | mixed N,O donor but still strong because chelation and deprotonation compensate for not being a pure hard O-donor |
| Salicylate / Tiron | aromatic O,O chelation and resonance stabilization make them stronger than simple monocarboxylates |
| Fluoride vs chloride | Fe(III) should prefer F− over Cl− by HSAB because fluoride is the harder base |

claims.text: ## Edge cases
| Hydroxide | strongest evidence in the retrieved data; Fe(III)-OH chemistry quickly becomes hydrolysis, olation/oxolation, and solids rather than simple soluble ML only |
| Thiocyanate | abundant in Fe(III) catalog despite not being a classic hard donor; ambidentate coordination and charge-transfer chemistry make it chemically prominent |
| 8-Hydroxyquinoline | mixed N,O donor but still strong because chelation and deprotonation compensate for not being a pure hard O-donor |
| Salicylate / Tiron | aromatic O,O chelation and resonance stabilization make them stronger than simple monocarboxylates |
| Fluoride vs chloride | Fe(III) should prefer F− over Cl− by HSAB because fluoride is the harder base |

paragraphs.text:
## Bottom line

The SRD-46 survey shows a clear hierarchy: **Fe(III) is most strongly stabilized by hard, anionic, multidentate O-donor ligands**, with hydroxamates, catecholate/Tiron-type ligands, and aminopolycarboxylates at the top; **simple carboxylates and monodentate halides/pseudohalides are weaker**. **Fe(II)** is generally less strongly bound by these hard O-donor classes and becomes relatively more competitive only with stronger-field neutral N-donor ligands. The ordering is best explained by **HSAB + ligand charge + chelate effect**, while **crystal-field effects** mainly account for the Fe(II) edge cases. The biggest exception is **hydroxide**, where very favorable Fe(III)–O interactions push the system into **hydrolysis, polynuclear species, and precipitation** rather than just larger simple formation constants.

If you want, I can do a tighter second pass focused only on **representative numerical log β values** for 6–10 named ligands, using narrower ligand-specific stability queries to build a cleaner comparison table for Fe(II) and Fe(III).

simple carboxylates and monodentate halides/pseudohalides are weaker
**Fe(II)** is generally less strongly bound by these hard O-donor classes
and becomes relatively more competitive only with stronger-field neutral N-donor ligands.
The ordering is best explained by **HSAB + ligand charge + chelate effect**
while **crystal-field effects** mainly account for the Fe(II) edge cases.
The biggest exception is **hydroxide**,
where very favorable Fe(III)–O interactions push the system into **hydrolysis, polynuclear species, and precipitation** rather than just larger simple formation constants.

If you want, I can do a tighter second pass focused only on **representative numerical log β values** for 6–10 named ligands, using narrower ligand-specific stability queries to build a cleaner comparison table for Fe(II) and Fe(III).
